title: Tables
assignment: Review the concepts from this section. If something is unclear, go back to the lesson.
groups.0.title: Rows and columns
groups.0.items.0.def: <table> is the table, <tr> is a row (table row), <td> is a cell (table data). You write row by row, left to right — exactly as you read. Spaces do not align; the browser swallows them.
groups.0.items.1.def: You write rows. Columns are a CONSEQUENCE — if every row has three cells, there are three columns. One row with four cells breaks the table and it shows at once.
groups.1.title: Heading and name
groups.1.items.0.def: The header cell (table header). The first row is not data — it is the NAMES of the columns. A blind person hears "Price: 18" and knows which column without counting. With <td> they hear only "18" and wonder what the number is.
groups.1.items.1.def: The name of the table itself — the first thing inside <table>, not above it. The page's heading does not do this job. Move the table elsewhere and it must still say what it holds and from when.
groups.2.title: When a table
groups.2.items.0.def: The test: take a cell from the middle — what does it MEAN? "18" = the price of the mountain bike (row + column). There is an answer → data, the table is right. A menu on the left, text on the right — what is their row? None → that is LOOK, not structure. Layout is CSS. A layout table lies: a blind person hears "table, three rows" and gets a site chopped into meaningless cells.
